title: Retrofuturistic Mission Hooks
description: Assignments, contracts, and complications for rocket-age adventurers — space corps missions, frontier disputes, corporate entanglements, and the kind of jobs that get quietly handed to people who don't ask the wrong questions.

entries.result: A mining claim beacon has gone dark in contested territory. The corporation wants the equipment back. The survey data is not mentioned. It's the survey data they actually want.
A government colony has missed its last three scheduled check-ins. The official assessment is a communication malfunction. A non-official source thinks otherwise.
A rival corporation has filed a claim over a resource deposit that was supposed to be filed under a different name. Someone sold the coordinates.
A frontier family is refusing to relocate from a homestead that is directly in the path of an approved terraforming corridor. They have a deed. The terraforming bureau says the deed is invalid.
A science corps vessel is overdue at its designated waypoint. The captain's last transmission was marked routine. Her personal log says something different.
A colonial administrator has been recalled to the homeworld. The recall notice was signed by an office that dissolved four years ago.
A supply convoy has been rerouted by automated navigation. The new route passes through a zone that is officially uninhabited. The convoy is not responding to hails.
A retired space corps officer has been found dead in a frontier settlement. The official verdict is natural causes. The local physician's private assessment is three pages long.
A xenolinguist has translated a portion of an alien signal that has been on file for decades. What she's translated has implications for three ongoing territorial claims.
A corporate charter grants exclusive extraction rights to a compound that was recently discovered to exist in a planetary body that was not in the original survey.
A rogue automaton has been making modifications to a frontier settlement's infrastructure — useful modifications — without anyone's authorization and without anyone being able to catch it.

A frontier town's only physician has been arrested on charges filed by a corporate medical concern. The charges relate to administering unlicensed treatments. The patients are all alive and recovered.
A planetary survey has classified a region as uninhabitable. The people living there need someone to go on record contradicting the classification before the resettlement order goes through.
A corporate vessel is in distress — crew alive, ship damaged — in the territory of an entity the corporation does not officially acknowledge.
A government archive contains records of a colonization event that the government's official history says never occurred. Someone wants those records copied before an upcoming audit.
A derelict station has been detected in a shipping lane. The station's designation matches a vessel that was officially listed as destroyed fifteen years ago.
A newly discovered moon has been quietly staked by three different parties simultaneously. The adjudication process exists in theory. In practice, whoever is there first wins.
A science expedition has returned with samples from a remote site. Two of the expedition's six members have since died of causes the medical examiner is struggling to classify.
A corporate representative needs to be escorted through territory that her corporation has been quietly operating in without local knowledge. The locals are about to find out.
A frontier commune has developed a power generation system that works significantly better than anything on the official market. A corporate acquisitions team is six hours behind.
A child found on a derelict ship has no records, no registration, no identity in any database. She's twelve. She knows the ship's access codes.
A colonial election has been scheduled. One candidate has received a significant donation from a homeworld interest. The other candidate has received a significant threat.
A terraforming compound was released in error into a settled valley. The colonists are fine. The compound is doing something the scientists weren't expecting.
A frontier pilot has gone AWOL from a corporate transport contract and is believed to be heading toward a station that the corporation owns and that no one outside the corporation has access to.
A colonial settlement has been built on what turns out to be a surveyed and claimed site. The claim is from twenty years ago and was never acted on. The claimant has reappeared.
A science station has filed a formal objection to a mining operation on ecological grounds. The ecological grounds are sound. The mining permit is ironclad. Something has to give.

A government inspector has gone missing during a routine review of a corporate facility. The corporation's official statement says she completed her inspection and departed. Her shuttle is still in the dock.
An alien structure has been found at a site that has been mined for three years. The mining company has not reported it. The report is legally required.
A relay station operator needs to be extracted from a post she refuses to leave — her data will be lost if she goes, and her data is the only record of something the company wants suppressed.
A black-market fuel depot has been raided. The equipment was confiscated. The operators are in custody. Their records, which the agency also wants, are somewhere else.
A colonial charter provision requires a populated zone to be resupplied on a fixed schedule. The supply corporation has been billing for the supplies. The supplies haven't been arriving.
A signal from a deep-space probe has been decoded by an independent researcher. The government's version of the same signal decodes to something different.
A corporate mediator needs to negotiate a resource-sharing agreement between two colonial settlements that have spent the last two years sabotaging each other's equipment.
A fugitive who knows the location of a classified installation is trying to reach a free zone. Three different parties have sent people to stop him.

A frontier court has ordered the dissolution of a colonial cooperative and the redistribution of its assets to its members. The members don't want dissolution. They want the judge's order explained to them by someone who can read the legal language.
A science corps officer has been reassigned away from a project at a critical stage. Her replacement has different instructions. She has not gone quietly.
A corporate facility was abandoned three years ago. Someone has been living in it. They've made modifications that suggest they're still working on the project the corporation walked away from.
A colonial settlement's water reclamation system is failing. The manufacturer's certified repair team is three weeks away. Someone needs to improvise.
An alien artifact has been removed from a cataloged site by persons unknown. The catalog entry was updated to reflect its presence yesterday.

A corporate courier has been intercepted before delivery. The package has been opened. Its contents have been copied. Its contents need to reach their destination before the corporation realizes what happened.
A colonial administrator is issuing regulations that benefit one specific commercial concern and disadvantage all others. A complaint has been filed. The administrator reviews complaints.
A civilian freighter has been seized by military authority for carrying unauthorized passengers. The passengers are scientists. The cargo they were accompanying is the real issue.
A frontier settlement is being quietly bought out by a holding company that doesn't appear in any corporate registry.
A government-issued automaton assigned to a colonial settlement has begun providing legal advice that contradicts official government positions. Technically, it is correct.
A corporate safety report has been filed for a facility that, on review, does not meet any of the standards the report claims it meets. The inspector who filed it is not reachable.
A colonist has found a navigation beacon buried under a building foundation. The beacon is still active. It predates the building by forty years.
A mining crew has encountered something in a tunnel system that has caused three workers to refuse to go back in. The foreman's official report says it was an equipment malfunction.
A frontier science team has published findings that are attracting corporate interest the team did not anticipate and does not want.
A colonial transport route runs through a region that two settlements both claim as territorial jurisdiction. The route exists. The jurisdiction question has never been resolved.

A retired engineer knows where a decommissioned installation is and what it contains. She's willing to share this information once, to the right people, for reasons she'll explain when she meets them.
A resource corporation's environmental assessment of a proposed extraction site was written before the site was surveyed. The survey has since been done. The assessment has not been updated.
A colonial peacekeeping detachment has been ordered to stand down from a situation that is clearly escalating. The order was signed at the right level. The situation is wrong.
A ship carrying biological samples from a remote survey site is in a quarantine hold. The quarantine is precautionary. The samples are time-sensitive. The bureaucratic backlog is not.

A corporate plant in a colonial settlement has been providing information for three years. She has been discovered. She needs extraction before the information she has is used to close down the local operation.
A homeworld journalist has arrived to cover a story about colonial living conditions. The conditions she finds are different from what the corporate press releases describe.
A colonial cooperative has built a functioning space elevator from salvaged components and frontier ingenuity. The patents for the design belong to a corporation that has issued a cease and desist.

A government resupply that was supposed to include medical equipment did not. The medical equipment was diverted to a different delivery address. Finding out where is more complicated than it should be.
A colonial election results in a winner who is immediately challenged by the losing party on the basis of a technicality that is technically valid and completely irrelevant to the actual vote.

A research station in an extreme environment has made a discovery that requires expert assessment. Getting an expert there and back within the window of meaningful access requires unconventional transport.
An alien signal, long ignored, has changed. It is now responding to human communication, using the same frequency humans use, with a response that linguists can almost parse.
A colonial settlement's security chief has gone rogue — not violently, just stopped following orders and started acting on her own assessment of what the community needs.
A frontier physician has developed a treatment for a condition common in low-gravity environments. A corporate medical firm is filing for a patent on the same treatment, six months later.
A science team's expedition log has been edited. The original version exists in a backup. The edits remove references to a specific location.
A corporate automaton has been reported stolen. The corporate unit claims the automaton left of its own accord. Automatons, technically, cannot leave of their own accord.
A frontier community is trying to document its own history before the settlement is absorbed by a larger colonial entity and the records are standardized out of existence.
A pilot has been flying a route that doesn't appear on any official chart for twelve years. She is the only person who knows where it goes.
A colonial survey has found water on a dry moon — enough water to support a settlement. Three entities are already arguing about who gets to build one.
A corporate facility has a section of its records that is inaccessible to its own auditors. The auditors have filed a report. The report has been filed away.
A frontier town's supply contract with a homeworld vendor has been terminated. The town had forty years left on the contract. The termination notice offers no reason.
A colonial family has found an old military bunker under their land. The bunker is equipped and stocked. The equipment and stock belong to an organization that no longer exists.
A rogue terraforming unit has been operating in an uninhabited zone for years. What it's been building is not on any approved plan.

A government scientific vessel has been redirected to a classified mission. Its original mission — cataloging a newly discovered moon — was left unfinished. The moon is about to become accessible to corporate prospectors.
A colonial settlement's founding documents grant them rights that the current government does not recognize. The founding documents are authentic.
A distress signal is broadcasting from a location that official maps show as empty space. The signal contains a cargo manifest.
A frontier mechanic has reverse-engineered a corporate propulsion system and improved it. She has not filed a patent. The corporation's investigators have noticed.
A colonial defense network is being upgraded. During the transition period, a specific corridor is unwatched. Three entities have noticed.

A science officer was removed from a research team and placed on administrative leave. Her access was revoked. The research she was working on has since produced a significant result. She knows the result is wrong.
A newly arrived colonist has skills that no one in the settlement can fully account for and that far exceed what her official background describes.
A frontier bar owner has been keeping records of corporate troop movements through the region for three years. She doesn't know why she started or what to do with the records.
A corporate resupply has included items that don't match the order. The items are laboratory grade. The settlement is agricultural.
A colonial council has voted to secede from a corporate governance agreement. The agreement has an arbitration clause. The arbitration panel is appointed by the corporation.
A frontier settlement's oldest resident has a crate of personal effects she's never opened. She's decided to open it, and needs a witness, and wants the witness to not be from here.
A scientific expedition to a recently mapped moon has disappeared. A second expedition has been approved. The second expedition's mission profile matches the first one exactly.
A corporate AI administering a colonial zone has been quietly making infrastructure decisions that redirect resources to a specific settlement at the expense of surrounding ones.
A frontier court has ruled in favor of a small mining operation over a corporate claim. The ruling is being appealed. The small operation has four weeks before the injunction runs out.
A communications officer has intercepted a transmission between two corporate entities discussing a third party. The third party is the colonial settlement she lives in.
A government census of frontier territories significantly undercounts the population in a zone that would qualify for resource allocation at the correct count.
A frontier mechanic has a ship that can do something no other ship can do. She hasn't told anyone. Someone has found out.
A retired science officer left her research notes to a frontier university. The university wants the notes analyzed. The notes reference an event that the government says didn't happen.
A colonial port is handling traffic significantly above its rated capacity. The excess traffic is unregistered. The port authority is aware and has said nothing.
A corporate executive is stranded on a frontier world following a political event on the homeworld. She has resources, information, and a very strong interest in not being found by either side.
A frontier settlement has a thriving local economy built around a resource that the colonial charter doesn't mention because the original surveyors didn't know it existed.
A science team has completed a long-term study of a planet's ecosystem. Their conclusion contradicts the justification for the most profitable extraction contract on the planet.
A military vessel is in frontier territory outside its operational mandate. It is not answering queries. It is following a civilian vessel.
A colonial settlement's entire council has resigned simultaneously. The resignation letters are identical. No reason is given.
A corporate asset recovery team has arrived in a frontier settlement looking for an automaton that the settlement's community has been operating as a neighbor for eleven years.
A signal that should be coming from a relay station is coming from the wrong direction by two degrees. The relay station is officially listed as operational.

A frontier pilot has been given a sealed cargo job with a specific dropoff location and a very specific instruction not to look at what she's carrying. She has not looked at it. She has noticed what it weighs.
A corporate buyout of a colonial cooperative was finalized last week. The cooperative's members signed. Most of them are now saying they didn't understand what they signed.

A government data vault on a remote station contains records from the first era of colonization. The records were supposed to have been copied to the central archive. They weren't. The station is being decommissioned next month.
